How to Design Your Own Learning
Another sharing post today. The reason: based on the three earlier posts —
How to Properly Learn Frontend Tech & Programming Languages
How Much Are You Willing to Sacrifice for What You Want
— a lot of people are asking me how to learn and take notes. So here's my advice.
Before reading this post, here's an important concept:
Someone else's learning style doesn't necessarily fit you.
Why? Say a beginner spends 16+ hours a day learning, from morning to night, and ships a major project every month. Do you think you could handle that?
Most people can't (I can't). So before reading further, hold this core idea: every person's learning style is unique. What you read or hear is just reference.
I'll split notes into two main categories.
When learning a syntax or tech, we usually record the key points the textbook / teacher emphasizes. E.g., for CSS Float, your note might look like this ↓
language-css shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
/* float has two main values, used to lay elements side by side */
/* clear works with float to clear the float effect */
And then? Nothing else, because that's the typical way most people take notes.
There's also two types of people:
People with a bit more skill
Common beginner issue: when learning new stuff, you feel

. Like memorizing a pile of inexplicable runes. That doesn't mean you didn't work hard — generally if you write that stuff down, you'll have an impression in the moment, but you'll forget it after a while.
For more advanced people, they pick things up fast and can mimic, but mimicry is mimicry — you can't really apply it.
The reason behind both: we're all using the way we learned in school.

Blindly copying notes, blindly following the pattern, blindly understanding what the teacher said. You might have a strong impression and great memory, but the stuff we're learning now isn't like school where there are midterms and finals with fixed answers. Usually you have to combine existing knowledge to produce a new answer.
I'll use the float property as the example. As I said, typical notes look like this ↓
How should review notes look? Simple. One idea:
explain it the way a regular person would understand
. That's it. Here's how I'd explain float.

"Float" in Chinese means "floating", so generally after applying the float property, the class "floats up" and the parent loses height. The parent loses height because its height is decided by content, so you need to pair it with
to pull the floated element back down and give the parent its height back.
Just text is this simple. But you can explain even better ↓
First I make a class called
language-HTML shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
is floated up. Drawn out, it looks like this (sorry for the bad drawing, QQ).
This is a floated element

So you'll notice review notes take more time and end up long. They take more time because you have to think about the principles and visualize how things work. But these notes help more than memory-point notes — they prove you actually got it. When writing them, watch:
Try to explain as fully as possible
Don't write too obscurely — even a beginner should be able to follow
A lot of people want a learning plan. Many learning styles exist — let me offer some directions.

What does "learn from problems" mean? You already have the basics, but you don't know how to combine them. So I throw a hard problem at you — most people don't know how to answer. Many skills are combination skills. Like HexSchool's final project — it's combining everything the teachers taught earlier.

To build that final project, you have to be familiar with what the teachers taught. So you have to circle back and shore up the basics. Did you ever think about why the teachers don't just teach you how to build the final project too?
Because the teachers want you to take a problem and learn from it — to cultivate learning.
If the teachers also taught you how to build the final, what you'd build is a copy of them — that doesn't cultivate a real learning plan.
If you find solutions yourself, you're actively applying the knowledge fragments the teachers gave you to compose your own learning plan.

There will be challenges. But if you never spend time understanding principles or problems, you can't actively apply what others taught to build something new. You'll drown in the knowledge sea and give up — or even avoid that whole domain.
For example: after I built my GitHub mini project, I wondered if I could build a GitLab version.
Notice that the teacher never asked me to build this — I made trouble for myself. I call this process:
Deliberately creating problems to learn from.

It was hard to find resources — I couldn't find a GitLab API doc. But because I'd created the problem, I had a direction. I just had to spend time hunting for answers. And those problems usually had sub-problems behind them, waiting to be solved.
When building it, I first had to find the API doc, then figure out how to read it. Otherwise how would I know how to integrate?
Once I solved integration, a new problem: how do I pull the data I want?
You'll notice I'm learning from problems, and from those problems I'm spawning more problems. Feels like a vast scary universe, right?
So I'm just creating problems for myself? When am I going to actually learn?
But you know what — if you do what I'm doing, you're already on the learning path. You also have your own learning plan. You're already past so many people.

The only learning that really counts as learning is what you explore yourself. But learning from problems will take you further and further, because your challenges are unknown. No famous expert ever says "I've learned everything!" — because the learning road is endlessly wide.
If you find my pitch unconvincing, here's another example.

When building my Vue.js final project, I used a lot of libraries and patterns the teacher didn't cover in class. I was also constantly creating trouble for myself. You can see the difference between V2 and V3 ↓
I wanted the site to feel closer to the game look, so I added a map navigation, more game-related copy, and even built an admin landing page ↓

I really created a lot of problems for myself. Building the admin home was the worst — the original API couldn't support it. But to make it work, I tried every approach I could think of. Dev was tough, but I learned how to actively use that API and the libraries.

After reading this, I hope you have a clearer direction for your learning plan and how to write notes. Hope it helps — let's grow together
